title.english: The Language of the Land: Whakapapa of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
title.teReoMāori: Te Reo o te Whenua: Te Whakapapa o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku

subject: Te Reo Māori
culturalContext: Students will explore how the Mangakōtukutuku stream embodies Ngāti Kahungunu whakapapa, connecting language to place. Through local pūrākau (stories) of the awa, they will understand how the stream's name reflects the ancestral connection to the kōtukutuku (tree fuchsia) and how its waters carry the reo of our tūpuna.
learningObjectives: Identify and use 10+ location-specific Māori kupu (e.g., awa, pari, puna, manga) related to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku in spoken and written sentences.
Recount the pūrākau of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku using appropriate sentence structures and sequencing language (i te wā, i mua, ā muri ake).
Create a simple pepeha that incorporates the awa as a significant landmark, introducing themselves in relation to it.
Explain in basic te reo Māori one reason why the awa is a taonga to Ngāti Kahungunu, using the conjunction 'nā te mea' (because).

activities.title: He Hīkoi, He Kōrero - A Walk, A Story
activities.description: Guided hīkoi to the banks of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. A local kaumātua will share the pūrākau of the awa, pointing out specific features (pari/cliff, puna/spring) and using the associated kupu. Students will use tablets to record audio of the story and take photos of key features.

activities.title: Ngā Kupu o te Awa - The Words of the Stream
activities.description: Back in the classroom, students work in pairs (huirau) to create a digital flashcards set (using Quizlet or similar) for the new location kupu learned. They must include the kupu, an image from the hīkoi, and a simple sentence using the word (e.g., 'Kei te rere te wai o te awa.' - The water of the stream is flowing).

activities.title: He Pakiwaitara ā-Kura - A School-based Story
activities.description: Using a storyboard template, groups sequence the main events of the awa's pūrākau. They will write 1-2 simple sentences in te reo Māori for each frame, focusing on correct word order and the sequencing language introduced (i te wā, i mua).

activities.title: Taku Awa, Taku Pepeha - My Stream, My Introduction
activities.description: Students draft and practice a personal pepeha. The standard template is expanded to include the line 'Ko Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku te awa', affirming their connection to this local taonga. They practice delivering it to a partner, focusing on clear pronunciation (whakahua tika).

activities.title: He Taonga Tuku Iho - A Treasure Handed Down
activities.description: A panel of 2-3 kaumātua from the local marae visit the class. Students, in groups, ask one prepared question in te reo about why the awa is a taonga (e.g., “He aha i taonga ai te awa?” - Why is the stream a treasure?). They then present their group's pepeha to the visitors.

resources: Kaumātua from Omāhu Marae (specifically, Hōri Reti, a known storyteller and fluent speaker)
The digital storymap 'Ngā Awa o Heretaunga' hosted on the Manaaki Whenua Landcare Research website
Copies of the book 'Ngā Pūrākau o Ngāti Kahungunu' by Dr. Emily Schuster
Tablets with audio recording and camera functions for the hīkoi

tasks: Submission of the digital flashcard set demonstrating understanding of 10+ location kupu.
Completion of the sequenced storyboard with 1-2 simple sentences in te reo per frame.
A video recording of the student delivering their final pepeha, including the line for Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku.

nzcAlignment: Te Reo Māori: Communication - Mana Reo (Level 4-5: Use relevant pronunciation, give personal information, understand and use familiar expressions.)
Te Reo Māori: Language Knowledge - Mana Reo (Level 4-5: Recognise that te reo Māori is a living language; Understand how language and culture are intertwined.)
Social Sciences: Identity, Culture, and Organisation - Level 5: Understand how cultural practices reflect and express people’s customs, traditions, and values.
